actions by declaring <em>entry/do/exit</em> keyword followed by a semicolon.</li><li>Define actions within the state by declaring <em>entry action/do action/exit action</em> keyword, <span>specify the element name</span>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Specializations" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[specialization]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (optional), then either end the element specification or specify the element body.</li><li>Specify actions referencing those defined elsewhere in the model by declaring <em>entry/do/exit</em> keyword followed by the name of the action you want to reference, then either end the element specification or specify the element body.</li></ol></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize<span> </span></strong>button.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a41a0a05-4dff-4d20-b128-1a7f3721498b"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span In the dropdown menu, select the element you want to create. The new element is created and displayed in its appropriate compartment.</li><li>Select a state symbol and on the right side of the <strong>actions</strong> compartment, click the<span> </span><strong>Create <span>Element </span></strong>button. In the displayed menu, select the needed action.<strong><span> </span></strong>The new element is created and displayed in the <strong>actions</strong> compartment.</li></ul></li></ul><h4>Specifying entry, do, and exit actions for states via the Specification panel</h4><p>To specify entry, do, and exit actions for states via the Specification panel</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification panel"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Specification panel" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the Properties section bar, click the <strong>Create Element</strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_element_in_specification_pane_button.png" /></ac:image> button. The shortcut menu appears. </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu. </span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the <strong>actions</strong> compartment section's bar, click the <strong>Create Element</strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_element_in_compartment_via_specification_pane_button.png" /></ac:image> button. </span>In the shortcut menu, select the needed action. <br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The element is created in the <strong>actions</strong> compartment.<br /></span></li></ol>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422960 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00324: (2026x) Enumeration

- page_id: `254422960`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422960/2026x+Enumeration
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:49:43.166+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Common elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An enumeration definition's instances are limited to a set of enumeration usages, which are the enumerated values of the enumeration definition. An enumeration usage is defined by an enumeration definition and is restricted to only the set of enumerated values specified in the definition.

##### Creating enumeration usage and definition elements

###### Creating enumeration usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

To create enumeration usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:
  1. *enum def*for an enumeration definition.
  2. *enum* for an enumeration usage. The declaration of an enumerated value within the body of an enumeration definition may omit the *enum* keyword.
2. Specify the element name.
3. (Optional) Specify the enumeration's features in the element body.
4. Click the Synchronize button. attributedefConditionLevel{ 
attributecolor:ConditionColor; 
}enumdefConditionColor{red;green;yellow; } 
// OR 
enumdefConditionColor{ 
enumred; 
enumgreen; 
enumyellow; 
} 
 
enumdefRiskLevel:>ConditionLevel{ 
enumlow{:>>color=ConditionColor::green; } 
enummedium{:>>color=ConditionColor::yellow; } 
enumhigh{:>>color=ConditionColor::red; } 
}

###### Creating enumeration usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

To create enumeration usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, select one of the following:
  1. enum def to create an enumeration definition element.
  2. enum to create an enumeration usage element.
3. Name the element.
4. (Optional) Specify the enumeration's features.

###### Creating enumeration usage elements via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Compartments' space=''] page.

To create enumeration usage elements via the symbol compartment in a view

- Do one of the following:
  - Select an enumeration definition element symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, click the enum element.
  - Select an enumeration symbol and on the right side of the enums compartment, click the Create enum button. The new element is created and displayed in the enums compartment.

###### Creating enumeration usage elements via the Specification panel

To create enumeration usage elements via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space=''] of an enumeration definition.
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the **enum**element in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the **enums**compartment section's bar, click the **Create enum**[IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element is created in the **enums** compartment.

###### Creating enumeration definition elements via the view palette

To create enumeration definition elements via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Items/Ports/Attributes group, click the **enum** def button to create an enumeration definition element.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the enumeration symbol.
3. (Optional) Specify the enumeration's features.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422653 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00325: (2026x) Environment options

- page_id: `254422653`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422653/2026x+Environment+options
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-11-10T17:47:17.664+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Project management > (2026x) Modeling tool environment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page provides information on the SysML v2-specific environment options you can use to customize your workflow.

##### Opening the Environment Options dialog

To open the **Environment Options** dialog

- From the Options menu, select Environment . Then select either SysML v2 or SysML v2 Textual Editor group.

##### Modifying environment options

To modify environment options

1. Open the **Environment Options** dialog .
2. Locate the option you want to modify. Use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Quick filter' space='MT']uick filter to find the needed option in the list. Filtering is performed in all available options, not only the selected option group. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']Click the **Show Description** button to display the environment option description section at the bottom of the dialog to read about the option functionality. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. An option value can be changed by typing a new value, setting a value to true / false , or selecting a value from the list. Click the **Reset to Defaults** button to restore default option values.
4. Click OK .

##### SysML v2 Environment Options

| Option | Default value | Value options | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| SysML v2 |  |  |  |
| General |  |  |  |
| Show Element Keyword | true | true/false | Set to true to display element keywords (e.g., part, action, actor) across the UI, such as in the Containment tree, Specification panel, and shortcut menu, while excluding views. |
| SysML v2 Textual Editor |  |  |  |
| Code Style |  |  |  |
| Add Blank Lines Between Textual Notation Blocks | false | true/false | Set to true to insert a blank line after each textual notation block in the textual notation to separate the blocks. |
| Display Specializations as Keywords | false | true/false | Set to true to display specializations as keywords rather than symbols in the textual notation. >]]> |
| Display Metadata as Keywords | false | true/false | Set to true to display metadata as keywords rather than symbols in the textual notation. >]]> |
| Use Short Name Instead of Name | true | true/false | Set to true to display the element's short name instead of its name in the textual notation. >]]>This option applies only when an element is targeted outside the Textual Editor (e.g., when subsetting an element in a view). If the element is directly targeted in the Textual Editor by typing its name, it will not be automatically replaced with the short name. However, if the element is first targeted in a view and then opened in the Textual Editor, the short name of the target element will be displayed. |
| Use Short Name Instead of Name for Requirement | false | true/false | Set to true to display the requirement's short name instead of its name in the textual notation. >]]>This option applies only when a requirement is targeted outside the Textual Editor (e.g., when subsetting a requirement in a view). If the requirement is directly targeted in the Textual Editor by typing its name, it will not be automatically replaced with the short name. However, if the requirement is first targeted in a view and then opened in the Textual Editor, the short name of the target requirement will be displayed. |
| Max Column for Hard Wrap | 170 | Number | Specify the maximum number of columns for hard wrapping lines in the Textual Editor. When a line exceeds the specified limit, it is split into sections of new indented lines to fit the line length based on the maximum number of columns. When wrapping, the editor follows specific rules, such as breaking at keywords ("connect", "from", "to", "of"), binary operators ("+", "-"), commas, and long single-line bodies. Set to -1 to disable the max column. |
| Editor |  |  |  |
| Synchronize Model with Errors | false | true/false | Set to true to synchronize the model with the textual notation, even if the notation contains errors. >]]> |
| Name Resolution Error Limit | 10 | Number | Specify the name resolution error limit to control the handling of name resolution errors. When specified, the name resolution stops if the error limit is reached, leaving the remaining names unresolved. This limit applies independently to each open Textual Editor tab. Set to -1 to disable the error limit and allow name resolution to continue for all names, skipping only those with errors. >]]> |
| Force KerML Namespaces Opening | false | true/false | Set to true to force the opening of KerML namespaces in the Textual Editor. |
| Preferred Element Name for Autocompletion | Short name | Short nameNameBoth names | Specify which element name should be suggested in context-sensitive autocompletion. If the selected name type is not specified for an element, autocompletion will suggest an existing name type instead. >]]> |
| Enable Sticky Header | true | true/false | Set to true to enable the sticky header in the Textual Editor, which displays the owner of the currently visible notation at the top of the editor. Reopen the Textual Editor for the changes to take effect. >]]> |
| Sticky Header Line Count | 5 | Number | Specify the maximum number of lines to display in the sticky header. Reopen the Textual Editor for the changes to take effect. >]]> |
| Font Size | 14 | Number | Specify the default font size. |
| Text Generation |  |  |  |
| Smart Generation Timeout | -1.0 | -1.0 - ∞ | Specify the timeout duration for smart generation in seconds. During textual notation generation, the tool generates the shortest qualified names. If the timeout is specified, a simpler name is generated once the time limit is reached. Set to -1 to disable the timeout. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Then select either <strong>SysML v2</strong> or <strong>SysML v2 Textual Editor </strong>group.</li></ul><h3>Modifying environment options</h3><p>To modify environment options</p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Environment Options dialog"><ac:link-body>Open the <strong>Environment Options</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Locate the option you want to modify.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7672be49-876c-41ce-9a52-f49c111fe1d2"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Use the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Quick filter" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[q]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>uick filter to find the needed option in the list. <strong>Show Description</strong> button to display the environment option description section at the bottom of the dialog to read about the option functionality.<br /><ac:image ac:title="The Show data-highlight-colour="#f4f5f7"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);" title="">General</span></td></tr><tr><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Show Element Keyword</span></td><td>true</td><td>true/false</td><td>Set to true to display element keywords (e.g., <em>part</em>, <em>action</em>, <em>actor</em>) across the UI, such as in the Containment tree, Specification panel, and shortcut menu, while excluding <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">views</span>.</td></tr><tr><td class="highlight-#deebff" title="Background color : Light blue 35%" colspan="4" data-highlight-colour="#deebff"><strong title="">SysML v2 Textual Editor</strong></td></tr><tr><td class="highlight-grey" title="Background color : " colspan="4" data-highlight-colour="grey">Code Style</td></tr><tr><td>Add Blank Lines Between Textual Notation Blocks</td><td>false</td><td>true/false</td><td>Set to true to insert a blank line after each textual notation block in the textual notation to separate the blocks.</td></tr><tr><td>Display Specializations as Keywords</td><td>false</td><td>true/false</td><td>Set to true to display specializations as keywords rather than symbols in the textual notation. <ac:link ac:anchor="Keywords"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Managing textual notation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></td></tr><tr><td>Display Metadata as Keywords</td><td>false</td><td>true/false</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Set to true to display metadata as keywords rather than symbols in the textual notation. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Metadata" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></div></td></tr><tr><td>Use Short Name Instead of Name</td><td>true</td><td>true/false</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Set to true to display the element's short name instead of its name in the textual notation. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Element names" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fdff2ca8-2aba-4707-bf44-d9357e0e7923"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This option applies only when an element is targeted outside the Textual Editor (e.g., when subsetting an element in a view). If the element is directly targeted in the Textual Editor by typing its name, it will not be automatically replaced with the short name. However, if the element is first targeted in a view and then opened in the Textual Editor, the short name of the target element will be displayed.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></div></td></tr><tr><td>Use Short Name Instead of Name for Requirement</td><td>false</td><td>true/false</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Set to true to display the requirement's short name instead of its name in the textual notation. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Element names" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0d32625b-caed-4639-a0fd-e4bf9deeedd4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This option applies only when a requirement is targeted outside the Textual Editor (e.g., when subsetting a requirement in a view). If the requirement is directly targeted in the Textual Editor by typing its name, it will not be automatically replaced with the short name. However, if the requirement is first targeted in a view and then opened in the Textual Editor, the short name of the target requirement will be displayed.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></div></td></tr><tr><td>Max Column for Hard Wrap</td><td>170</td><td>Number</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Specify the maximum number of columns for hard wrapping lines in the Textual Editor. When a line exceeds the specified limit, it is split into sections of new indented lines to fit the line length based on the maximum number of columns. When wrapping, the editor follows specific rules, such as breaking at keywords (&quot;connect&quot;, &quot;from&quot;, &quot;to&quot;, &quot;of&quot;), binary operators (&quot;+&quot;, &quot;-&quot;), commas, and long single-line bodies. Set to -1 to disable the max column.</p></div></td></tr><tr><td class="highlight-grey" title="Background color : " colspan="4" data-highlight-colour="grey">Editor</td></tr><tr><td>Synchronize Model with Errors</td><td>false</td><td>true/false</td><td><p>Set to true to synchronize the model with the textual notation, even if the notation contains errors. <ac:link ac:anchor="Saving changes in the Textual Editor"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Managing Textual Editor" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></td></tr><tr><td>Name Resolution Error Limit</td><td>10</td><td>Number</td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Specify the name resolution error limit to control the handling of name resolution errors. When specified, the name resolution stops if the error limit is reached, leaving the remaining names unresolved. This limit applies independently to each open Textual Editor tab. Set to -1 to disable the error limit and allow name resolution to continue for all names, skipping only those with errors. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Managing textual notation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Force KerML Namespaces Opening</td><td>false</td><td>true/false</td><td>Set to true to force the opening of KerML namespaces in the Textual Editor.</td></tr><tr><td>Preferred Element Name for Autocompletion</td><td>Short name</td><td><p>Short name</p><p>Name</p><p>Both names</p></td><td>Specify which element name should be suggested in context-sensitive autocompletion. If the selected name type is not specified for an element, autocompletion will suggest an existing name type instead. <ac:link ac:anchor="Autocompletion"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Textual Editor features" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></td></tr><tr><td>Enable Sticky Header</td><td>true</td><td>true/false</td><td><p>Set to true to enable the sticky header in the Textual Editor, which displays the owner of the currently visible notation at the top of the editor. Reopen the Textual Editor for the changes to take effect. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Working with Textual Editor" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></td></tr><tr><td>Sticky Header Line Count</td><td>5</td><td>Number</td><td>Specify the maximum number of lines to display in the sticky header. Reopen the Textual Editor for the changes to take effect. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Working with Textual Editor" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></td></tr><tr><td>Font Size</td><td>14</td><td>Number</td><td>Specify the default font size.</td></tr><tr><td class="highlight-grey" title="Background color : " colspan="4" data-highlight-colour="grey">Text Generation</td></tr><tr><td>Smart Generation Timeout </td><td>-1.0</td><td>-1.0 - ∞</td><td>Specify the timeout duration for smart generation in seconds. During textual notation generation, the tool generates the shortest qualified names. If the timeout is specified, a simpler name is generated once the time limit is reached. Set to -1 to disable the timeout.</td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422605 space=SYSML2P version=8 -->
## PAGE 00326: (2026x) Exhibit state

- page_id: `254422605`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422605/2026x+Exhibit+state
- version_number: 8
- version_date: `2025-12-17T15:41:58.509+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) States
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An exhibit state usage specifies that its owner exhibits a state specified by the exhibit state usage.

An exhibit state usage can be a feature of:

- a part definition or usage, specifying that the referenced state is exhibited by the containing part.
- a state definition or usage, whereby the exhibit state usage represents a "call" from the containing state to the exhibited state.

There are two ways a state can be exhibited by another element:

50%

- by referencing the state usage defined elsewhere in the model.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the exhibit keyword (unnamed element) or the exhibit state keyword (named element) . [ATTACHMENT filename='exhibit_symbol.png'] [ATTACHMENT filename='exhibit_state_symbol_2.png']
  - See the procedures:
    - :
      - 
      - 
      - 

50%

- by containing an owned exhibit state usage, i.e., the exhibited state is the exhibit state usage itself.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the exhibit state keyword. [ATTACHMENT filename='exhibit_state_symbol.png']
  - See the procedures:
    - :
      - 
      - 
      - 

##### Exhibiting a state by referencing a state usage defined elsewhere in the model

###### Exhibiting states via the Textual Editor1Exhibiting states via the Textual Editor

To exhibit states via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of the element that you want to exhibit a state, do one of the following:
  1. For an unnamed exhibit state, declare the exhibit keyword followed by the name of the exhibited state.
  2. For a named one, declare the exhibit state keyword, specify the element name, then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Reference subsetting' space=''] the exhibited state.
2. Click the Synchronize button.

state vehicleStates; // referenced state

partvehicle{ 
// unnamed exhibit state referencing a state defined elsewhere in the model 
exhibitvehicleStates;

// named exhibit state referencing a state defined elsewhere in the modelexhibitstateoperatingStatereferences vehicleStates;// ORexhibitstateoperatingState::> vehicleStates;

}

###### Exhibiting states via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To exhibit states via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag a state element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Drag and Drop handler' space=''] and drop it on:
  - the target element symbol that you want to exhibit the state by referencing it using the **Create Exhibit State** command.
  - the exhibit state usage symbol that you want to exhibit the state by referencing it using the **Set Exhibited State** command.

###### Exhibiting statesvia the Specification panel

To exhibit states via the Specification panel

- Drag and drop a state element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Drag and Drop handler' space=''] and drop it on the **Exhibited State** field in the Properties section of the Specification panel for the exhibit state element. The appropriate state is specified for the element.

##### Exhibiting a statevia the ownedexhibited stateusageitselfas theexhibited state

###### Exhibiting states via the Textual Editor

To exhibit states via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of the element that you want to exhibit a state, declare the exhibit state keyword followed by its name.
2. Click the Synchronize button.

partvehicle{

// exhibited state is the defined exhibit state usage itself contained by the parentexhibitstate vehicleStates;}

###### Exhibiting states via the Create Element command

To exhibit states via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, select the exhibit state element.
3. Name the element.
4. Specify the exhibit state's features.

###### Exhibiting states via the view palette

To exhibit states via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the States group, click the exhibit state button.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the exhibit state symbol.
3. Name the element.
4. Specify the exhibit state's features.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254423168 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00327: (2026x) Exposing elements for views

- page_id: `254423168`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254423168/2026x+Exposing+elements+for+views
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-12-03T10:30:58.229+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Views
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once you have[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating views' space=''], you can specify what model elements can be displayed by the view using the expose relationship. The exposed elements can be further narrowed down using[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Filtering elements for views' space=''].

Elements can be exposed for views only via the Textual Editor. For displaying exposed elements on views, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in views' space=''] page.

##### Element exposition kinds and visibility

Expose relationship is a kind of import relationship and is thus declared like an import relationship, but using the keyword *expose* instead of *import*. However, unlike imports, whose visibility can be modified, expose relationships always have *protected* visibility, which means that the elements exposed by a view usage are not publicly visible outside the view usage.

The exposition kind is specified as a specific suffix added to the exposed elements qualified name.

| Exposition kind | Suffix | Exposed elements | Example |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Membership exposition | none | Only the specified namespace (i.e., element import). | part vehicleInformation { part vehicleSpecs {} part vehicleRecord { item vehicleModel; }}view 'Vehicle Details' { // membership exposition: expose vehicleInformation; // exposes vehicleInformation} |
| Namespace exposition | ::* | The content of the specified namespace. | part vehicleInformation { part vehicleSpecs {} part vehicleRecord { item vehicleModel; }}view 'Vehicle Details' { // namespace exposition: expose vehicleInformation::*; // exposes vehicleSpecs, vehicleRecord} |
| Recursive membership exposition | ::** | The specified namespace and its content, including sub-namespaces and their elements. | part vehicleInformation { part vehicleSpecs {} part vehicleRecord { item vehicleModel; }}view 'Vehicle Details' { // recursive membership exposition: expose vehicleInformation::**; // exposes vehicleInformation, vehicleSpecs, vehicleRecord, vehicleModel} |
| Recursive namespace exposition | ::*::** | The content of the specified namespace, including sub-namespaces and their elements. | part vehicleInformation { part vehicleSpecs {} part vehicleRecord { item vehicleModel; }}view 'Vehicle Details' { // recursive namespace exposition: expose vehicleInformation::*::**; // exposes vehicleSpecs, vehicleRecord, vehicleModel} |

##### Exposing elements

To expose elements

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor within the body of the view element for which you want to expose the element(s).
2. Declare the keyword expose .
3. Specify the qualified name of the membership/namespace you want to import, followed by the .
4. (Optional) Specify an element [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Filtering elements for views' space=''] .
5. Click the Synchronize button.

You can specify multiple *expose* relationships for the same view.

partvehicleInformation{partvehicleSpecs{}partvehicleRecord{itemvehicleModel;}}

view'Vehicle Details':DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv,DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::NonStandardLibraryElementFilter{exposevehicleInformation::**;// recursive membership exposition of *vehicleInformation*, *vehicleSpecs*, *vehicleRecord*, *vehicleModel*}

#### NOTE: Reviewing exposed elements

Reviewing exposed elements

To review the elements exposed for a specific element, open its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification window' space='']**Specification** window and check the values of the **Exposed Element**property.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Once you have </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[created a view]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, you can specify what model elements can be displayed by the view using the expose relationship. The exposed elements can be further narrowed down using </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Filtering elements for views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[filters]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d6246f2c-41c5-458e-aeb6-b5154a931bc6"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Elements can be exposed for views only via the Textual Editor. For displaying exposed elements on views, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Displaying elements in views]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Element exposition kinds and visibility</h3><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="0d5edfcf-b067-4682-bc97-2302f3d87a14">Expose relationship is a kind of import</ac:inline-comment-marker> relationship and is thus declared like an import relationship, but using the keyword <em>expose</em> instead of <em>import</em>. However, unlike imports, whose visibility can be modified, expose relationships always have <em>protected</em> visibility, which means that the elements exposed by a view usage are not publicly visible outside the view usage.</p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="8f5597dc-b1b5-4168-bb00-d486630aff9a">The exposition kind is specified as a specific suffix added to the exposed elements qualified name.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 78.761%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 19.9701%;" /><col style="width: 6.70997%;" /><col style="width: /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[filter]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">. </span></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize<span> </span></strong>button.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="efcc1e3b-c2f7-4ea1-9e85-5004051fb6b6"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422242 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
````
## PAGE 00328: (2026x) Extract Definition and Usage

- page_id: `254422242`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422242/2026x+Extract+Definition+and+Usage
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-12-17T15:01:34.512+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Element management > (2026x) Working with elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Over time, your model may grow to contain usage elements with repetitive features, thus the use of typing by definition elements would be useful. The Extract Definition command allows you to extract a definition element with the features of the usage element. This improves feature reusability, allowing you to easily generate definition elements that can be used for typing other usage elements.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']On the left: the same attributes *diameter*and *width* are each created for the parts *leftFrontWheel*and *rightFrontWheel*, resulting in duplicated elements that serve the same purpose. 
On the right: once the Extract Definition command is executed on the *rightFrontWheel*element, the definition is extracted with the part's attributes. 
The *rightFrontWheel*element is then defined by the extracted definition, inheriting its attributes. The *leftFrontWheel*part can then be defined by the definition as well.

##### Extracting a definition element from a usage element

Once you execute the Extract Definition command, all owned features, except for the redefined ones, are moved to the definition, which is then set as the type for the usage. All features that have been moved are then inherited instead of being owned by the usage.

To extract a definition element from a usage element

- Right-click a usage element(s) or its symbol(s) and in the shortcut menu select Refactor , then Extract Definition . The appropriate definition element is created.

##### Extracting a usage element

You can also use the Extract Usage command to extract a usage element. This is especially useful when reusing and adapting [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modeling actions with swimlanes' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Use case scenario' space=''], and states to different contexts.

Once you execute the Extract Usage command:

- The extracted usage is owned by the same package and named after the element from which it was extracted;
- The extracting usage reference subsets the extracted usage;
- All owned features of the extracting usage are moved to the extracted usage, except for the redefined ones;
- All features that have been moved are then inherited instead of owned by the extracting usage.

**Extract Usage**command is only available for [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Perform action' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Include use case' space=''], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Exhibit state' space=''], unless they reference other elements.

To extract a usage element from a perform action usage, include use case usage, or exhibit state usage element

- Right-click a perform action usage, include use case usage, or exhibit state usage element(s) or its symbol(s), and in the shortcut menu select Refactor , then Extract Usage . The appropriate action, use case, or state usage element is created.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Over time, your model may grow to contain usage elements with repetitive features, thus the use of typing by definition elements would be useful. The Extract Definition command allows you to extract a definition element with the features of the usage element. This improves feature reusability, allowing you to easily generate definition elements that can be used for typing other usage elements.<br /></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="extract_definition.png" /></ac:image>On the left: the same attributes <em>diameter </em>and <em>width</em> are each created for the parts <em>leftFrontWheel </em>and <em>rightFrontWheel</em>, resulting in duplicated elements that serve the same purpose.<br />On the right: once the Extract Definition command is executed on the <em>rightFrontWheel </em>element, the definition is extracted with the part's attributes. <br />The <em>rightFrontWheel </em>element is then defined by the extracted definition, inheriting its attributes. The <em>leftFrontWheel </em>part can then be defined by the definition as well.</h6><h3>Extracting a definition element from a usage element</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Once you execute the Extract Definition command, all owned features, except for the redefined ones, are moved to the definition, which is then set as the type for the usage. All features that have been moved are then inherited instead of being owned by the usage.</span></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To extract a definition element from a usage element</span></p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click a usage element(s) or its symbol(s) and in the shortcut menu select <strong>Refactor</strong>, then <strong>Extract Definition</strong>.<br />The appropriate definition element is created.</li></ul><h3>Extracting a usage element</h3><p>You can also use the Extract Usage command to extract a usage element. This is especially useful when reusing and adapting <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Modeling actions with swimlanes" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[actions]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Use case scenario" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[use cases]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and states to different contexts. </p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Once you execute the Extract Usage command:</span></p><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">The extracted usage is owned by the same package and <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2e95f070-07b9-4018-ac6d-93d219a560bf">named</ac:inline-comment-marker> after the element from which it was extracted;</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">The extracting usage reference subsets the extracted usage;</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">All owned features of the extracting usage are moved to the extracted usage, except for the redefined ones;</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">All features that have been moved are then inherited instead of owned by the extracting usage.<br /></span></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a02bf0be-16e5-4f6a-8a7d-684c9de0668b"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Extract Usage </strong>command is only available for <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Perform action" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[perform action]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Include use case" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[include use case]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Exhibit state" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[exhibit state usage]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, unless they <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">reference other elements.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To extract a usage element from a perform action usage, include use case usage, or exhibit state usage element</span></p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click a <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">perform action usage, include use case usage, or exhibit state usage </span>element(s) or its symbol(s), and in the shortcut menu select <strong>Refactor</strong>, then <strong>Extract Usage</strong>.<br />The appropriate action, use case, or state usage element is created.</li></ul><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422964 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00329: (2026x) Feature membership

- page_id: `254422964`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422964/2026x+Feature+membership
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:50:44.922+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Relations
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Use feature memberships to specify element features.

- Feature membership relationship is created automatically in the tool when you create a feature for an element.
  - For element creation procedures, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Managing elements' space=''] page. You can also create features through the creation of a feature membership itself. This is especially useful for element decomposition, such as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Part decomposition' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Requirement decomposition' space=''].
- For element ownership change procedures, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Element ownership change' space=''] page.
- The feature membership display can differ based on the related elements. If one of the elements related via a feature membership is a referential usage, it is displayed with a hollow diamond icon, whereas a composite usage is displayed with a filled diamond icon. For procedures on referential usages, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modifiers' space=''] section on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modifiers' space=''] page.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Specifying feature memberships

###### Specifying feature memberships via the Textual Editor

To specify feature memberships via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, use curly braces after element declaration to specify the element body. Features specified within the element body become related to the owning element via the feature membership. To indicate a referential (non-composite) usage, use the keyword *ref*before the element keyword. For more procedures on referential usages, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modifiers' space=''] section on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modifiers' space=''] page.
2. Click the Synchronize button. part'input unit'{ 
partmouse;// composite usagerefpartmonitor;// referential (non-composite) usage}

###### Specifying feature memberships via the smart manipulator in a view

Creating features via the smart manipulator is only available for a limited selection of elements (e.g., parts, requirements).

To specify feature memberships via the smart manipulator in a view

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating parts' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] the part for which you want to specify a feature membership.
2. In the selected symbol's smart manipulator, click the feature membership icon and do one of the following:
  1. To create a composite feature, click elsewhere in the view pane. The new composite feature of the same type is created.
  2. To designate an existing element as a composite one, click on the element you want to designate as the composite feature.

###### Specifying feature memberships via the view palette

To specify feature memberships via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the **feature membership** button.
2. In a view, click on the element symbol you want to designate as the owner, then the one you want to designate as the owned feature. A feature membership path symbol is created between the elements.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

This is especially useful for element decomposition, such as <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Part decomposition" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[part decomposition]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Requirement decomposition" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[requirement decomposition]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul></li><li>For element ownership change procedures, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Element ownership change" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Element ownership change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li><li><span>The feature membership display can differ based on the related elements. If one of the elements related via a feature membership is a <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2c31701e-4275-4971-8e9b-c06657bb835a">referential usage, it is displayed with a hollow diamond icon</ac:inline-comment-marker>, whereas a composite usage is displayed with a filled diamond icon. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422297 space=SYSML2P version=5 -->
## PAGE 00330: (2026x) Feature typing

- page_id: `254422297`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422297/2026x+Feature+typing
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:51:04.851+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Relations > (2026x) Specializations
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Use thefeature typing relationship tospecify a definition for a usage.

##### Feature typing

###### Defining an element via the Textual Editor

To define an element via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, identify the usage element that you want to define.
2. Follow the element name with either one of the following:
  1. keyword defined by
  2. textual symbol : (colon)
3. Follow the keyword with the name of the definition element you want to be the defining one.
4. Click the Synchronize button. partdieselEngine:Engine; 
/* or */ 
partdieselEnginedefined byEngine;

###### Defining an element via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To define an element via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Drag and Drop handler' space=''] on an element symbol executing the Set Type command.

###### Defining an element via the view palette

To define an element via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Specializations group, select feature typing .
2. Draw the line with the arrowhead going from the element you want to be defined to the defining one. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Defining an element via the Create Relationship command

To define an elementvia the Create Relationship command

1. Right-click the element that you want to define.
2. In the shortcut menu, click Create Relationship , then in the submenu, click Outgoing , and then in the submenu, click feature typing . The Element Picker dialog opens.
3. In the Element Picker dialog, do one of the following:
  1. In a view, click the element symbol you want to be the defining one. Hold Shift to select multiple elements.
  2. Click the Add [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to be the defining one and click OK .
  3. Drag and drop the needed element you want to be the defining one from the Containment tree onto the Element Picker .
4. In the Element Picker dialog, click OK .

###### Defining an element via the Specification panel

To define an element via the Specification panel

- See the procedure [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space=''] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE


<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254423172 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00331: (2026x) Filtering elements for views

- page_id: `254423172`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254423172/2026x+Filtering+elements+for+views
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:25:57.410+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Views
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can specify filters for view definitions and/or specific views to narrow down what elements can be displayed by views. Once you have[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating views' space=''], specified the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Exposing elements for views' space=''], you can then optionally filter the exposed elements. Views have the capability to filter elements upon exposition based on certain conditions.Only elements that meet all filter conditions are exposed for the view.

Elements can be filtered for views only via the Textual Editor.

##### Filtering elements for views

Ways to perform element filtering for views:

- . You can specify filter conditions directly for the specific view, using the keyword filter .
- . You can also filter elements for views through inheritance, e.g., if the view is defined by a view definition that contains filter conditions, the filters are inherited by and applied to the defined view.

###### Specifying filter conditions

- Filter conditions can be used only for [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Package' space=''] ([CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Importing and filtering elements' space='']) and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Views basics' space=''] (element exposition) elements.
- Filter condition expression must be model-level evaluable. Any SysML v2 expression can be used for the filter condition specification, but it must return *true/false*.
- @ , istype , and hastype classification operators are commonly used to check if an element has the given metadata annotation.
- Element metaclass metadatas are located in the used projects at Standard Libraries > SysML > Systems . Filter conditions require a qualified name for metadata-based filtering (e.g., @SysML::Systems::PartUsage ).

To specify filter conditions

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor within the body of the view.
2. Declare the keyword filter followed by filter condition expression, including the needed operator and the qualified name of the element you want to expose .
3. Click the Synchronize button.

packageMetadataDefinitions{metadatadefSafety{attributeisMandatory:Boolean;}}

packageVehicle{ 
privateimportMetadataDefinitions::**; 
partvehicle{ 
partinterior{ 
partalarm; 
partseatBelt[2] {@Safety{isMandatory=true;}} 
partfrontSeat[2]; 
partdriverAirBag{@Safety{isMandatory=false;}} 
} 
} 
}

view'Vehicle Features'{ 
exposeVehicle::**;// exposes package *Vehicle*// you can use multiple filter condition declarations: 
filter@SysML::Systems::PartUsage;// exposes part usage elements from the package *Vehicle* 
filter@MetadataDefinitions::SafetyandMetadataDefinitions::Safety::isMandatory;// exposes elements with *Safety* metadata attribute *isMandatory*value *true*from previously filtered part usage elements exposed from the package *Vehicle* 
}

###### Inheriting filter conditions

**Filters in predefined view definitions:**

- Standard predefined [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Symbolic views' space='']/[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Tabular views' space=''] view definitions (except for *RequirementsTable*) do not have any specified filters, meaning, any elements can be exposed by these views. Once you have created such a view, you may want to specify filters to limit the displayed elements.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Symbolic views' space=''] contain:
  - Element filters specified in the *DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::NonStandardLibraryElementFilter*and *DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::EssentialElementsFilter*view definitions, narrowing down what elements can be exposed by the view by discarding various standard library elements and other non-essential elements that are unlikely to be relevant for display in views.
  - Additionally, most of the predefined symbolic view definitions that use expressions contain additional filters to narrow down the exposed elements, e.g., *PartsTreeView*view definition contains filters specifying that only part usage elements can be exposed.

**Recommendations**:

- The easiest way to filter exposed elements is to reuse predefined symbolic view definitions that use expressions. See *Example 1*.
  - Keep in mind that the renderers specified for the view definitions are also inherited.
- If you only want to discard uncommon library elements from being displayed in a view, you can reuse the *DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::NonStandardLibraryElementFilter*and *DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::EssentialElementsFilter***view definitions. See *Example 2*.

#### PANEL: Example 1

Example 1

viewdefNonStandardLibraryElementFilter { 
// see the *DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::NonStandardLibraryElementFilter* view definition in the modeling tool to view all filters specified for it 
} 
viewdefTreeView :> EssentialElementsFilter, NonStandardLibraryElementFilter { // the *TreeView*view definition subclassifies the two view definitions inheriting their filters 
renderViews::asTreeDiagram; // the specified renderer will be inherited 
}

viewdefPartsTreeView :> TreeView { // the *PartsTreeView*view definition subclassifies *TreeView* inheriting all filters and renderer 
filterhastypePartUsage; 
}

viewnewView:DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::PartsTreeView{ // the specific view is defined by *PartsTreeView*view definition inheriting all filters and renderer 
exposeVehicle::**; 
}

#### PANEL: Example 2

Example 2

viewdefNonStandardLibraryElementFilter { 
// see the *DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::NonStandardLibraryElementFilter* view definition in the modeling tool to view all filters specified for it 
}

viewnewView: DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::EssentialElementsFilter, DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::NonStandardLibraryElementFilter{ // the specific view is defined by the view definitions inheriting their filters 
exposeVehicle::**; 
}

// the view is defined by the *Interconnection View* view definition (inheriting its renderer) and view definitions specifying filters:viewnewView: DS_Views::SymbolicViews::iv, DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::EssentialElementsFilter, DS_Views::SymbolicViewsByExpression::NonStandardLibraryElementFilter{exposeVehicle::**;}

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">You can specify filters for view definitions and/or specific views to narrow down what elements can be displayed by views. Once you have </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[created a view]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, specified the </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Exposing elements for views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[exposed elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, you can then optionally filter the exposed elements. Views have the capability to filter elements upon exposition based on certain conditions</span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="f2c9d056-bec4-4cef-bb45-c28f35571c82">.</ac:inline-comment-marker><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> Only elements that meet all filter conditions are exposed for the view. </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="633898d6-ce20-401d-a553-a6f554ba34a0"><ac:rich-text-body>Elements can be filtered for views only via the Textual Editor.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Filtering elements for views</h3><p>Ways to perform element filtering for views:</p><ul><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Specifying filter conditions" />. You can specify filter conditions directly for the specific view, using the keyword <em>filter</em><span>.</span></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Inheriting filter conditions" />. You can also filter elements for views through inheritance, e.g., if the view is defined by a view definition that contains filter conditions, the filters are inherited by and applied to the defined view. </li></ul><h4>Specifying filter conditions</h4><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2e981b38-7a7e-4fd8-9868-448d2c5db34b"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><span>Filter conditions can be used only for <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Package" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[package]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (<ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Importing and filtering elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[element import]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>) and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Views basics" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[view]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (element exposition) elements.</span></li><li>Filter condition expression must be model-level evaluable. <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c628d16d-3d4b-4e18-b3f5-2e6883130e10">Any SysML v2 expression can be used for the filter condition specification, but it must return <em>true/false</em>.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li><em>@</em>, <em>istype</em>, and <em>hastype</em> classification operators are commonly used to check if an element has the given metadata annotation.</li><li><span>Element metaclass metadatas are located in the used projects at </span><em style="text-align: left;">Standard Libraries &gt; SysML &gt; Systems</em><em style="text-align: left;">. </em>Filter conditions require a qualified name for metadata-based filtering (e.g., <em>@SysML::Systems::PartUsage</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">).</span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>To specify filter conditions </span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, place the cursor within the body of the view.</li><li>Declare the keyword<span> </span><em>filter </em>followed by filter condition expression, including the needed operator and the qualified name of the element you want to expose<span>.</span></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">expose</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Vehicle</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::**;<br /></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422191 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00332: (2026x) Find dialog

- page_id: `254422191`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422191/2026x+Find+dialog
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:06:51.780+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model management > (2026x) Working with Textual Editor > (2026x) Textual Editor features
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can invoke the Find dialog to search for or replace specific data in the textual notation.

##### Invoking the Find dialog

To invoke the Find dialog

- Use the Ctrl+F shortcut keys to invoke the Find dialog.

##### Searching for data in the textual notation via the Find dialog

To search for data in the textual notation via the Find dialog

1. Invoke the Find dialog.
2. In the Find field, enter the word you are searching for. The found instances are highlighted throughout the notation.

##### Replacing data in the textual notation via the Find dialog

You can use the Find dialog to replace specific data in the textual notation.

To replace data in the textual notation via the Find dialog

1. Invoke the Find dialog.
2. In the Find field, input the word you want to replace.
3. On the left side of the dialog, click the right-facing arrow. The Find dialog is expanded.
4. In the Replace field, input the replacement word.
5. Do one of the following:
  1. To replace the highlighted instance of the word, either click the Replace button on the dialog or Enter on the keyboard.
  2. To replace all instances of the word, either click the **Replace** **All**button on the dialog or Ctrl+Alt+Enter on the keyboard. All instances of the word can also be changed via the **Change All Occurrences**(Ctrl+F2) command.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p style="text-align: left;">You can invoke the Find dialog to search for or replace specific data in the textual notation. </p><h3>Invoking the Find dialog</h3><p style="text-align: left;">To invoke the Find dialog</p><hr /><ul><li>Use the Ctrl+F shortcut keys to invoke the Find dialog.</li></ul><h3>Searching for data in the textual notation via the Find dialog</h3><p>To search for data in the textual notation via the Find dialog</p><hr /><ol><li>Invoke the Find dialog.</li><li>In the <strong>Find</strong> field, enter the word you are searching for.<br />The found instances are highlighted throughout the notation.</li></ol><h3>Replacing data in the textual notation via the Find dialog</h3><p>You can use the Find dialog to replace specific data in the textual notation. </p><p>To replace data in the textual notation via the Find dialog</p><hr /><ol><li>Invoke the Find dialog.</li><li>In the <strong>Find</strong> field, input the word you want to replace.</li><li>On the left side of the dialog, click the right-facing arrow. The Find dialog is expanded.</li><li>In the <strong>Replace</strong> field, input the replacement word.</li><li>Do one of the following:<ol><li>To replace the highlighted instance of the word, either click the <strong>Replace</strong> button on the dialog or Enter on the keyboard.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">To replace all instances of the word, either click the <strong>Replace</strong> <strong>All </strong>button on the dialog or Ctrl+Alt+Enter on the keyboard.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a00e8947-6778-4457-a9d6-a064b6f6f518"><ac:rich-text-body><p>All instances of the word can also be changed via the <strong>Change All Occurrences </strong>(Ctrl+F2) command.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422266 space=SYSML2P version=6 -->
## PAGE 00333: (2026x) Flow

- page_id: `254422266`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422266/2026x+Flow
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2025-12-17T15:12:21.484+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Relations > (2026x) Connectors
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A flow is a kind of connection that specifies a transfer from a source output to a target input (and, optionally, the payload). A flow is a transfer that continues while the source and target actions execute. The transferred payload can be anything (attribute value, item, part, etc.).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The image displays the *fuelFlow* flow connectorfrom *fuelTank.fuelOut*to*engine.fuelIn*with the payload *flowingFuel*defined by *Fuel*.Additionally, the connector is also [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''].

##### Creating flow usage and definition elements

###### Creating flow usage elements via the Textual Editor

To create flow usage elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the flow keyword.
2. Specify the element name (optional), [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specializations' space=''] (optional), (optional), then the from keyword and the name of the source element and output feature. Then follow with the keyword to and the name of the target element and input feature. If the flow has no name and no specialization, the keyword *from*can be omitted.
3. Click the Synchronize button. flow fuelFlow:FuelFlowfromfuelTank.fuelOuttoengine.fuelIn; // named and specialized (feature typing) flowflow:FuelFlowfromfuelTank.fuelOuttoengine.fuelIn; // unnamed specialized(feature typing) flowflow fuelFlowfromfuelTank.fuelOuttoengine.fuelIn;// named unspecializedflowflowfuelTank.fuelOutto engine.fuelIn; // unnamed unspecializedflow can omit the keyword *from*

###### Creating flow usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view

To create flow usage elements via the smart manipulator in a view

1. In a view, select an element symbol, and in its smart manipulator, click the flow command.
2. A dropdown menu appears. Select the output element in the menu.
3. Click on the target element. A dropdown menu appears. Select the compatible input element in the menu. A flow usage is created between the elements.

###### Creating flow definition and usage elements via the view palette

To create flow definition and usage elements via the view palette

- To create a flow definition:
  1. In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the **flow** def button to create a flow definition element.
  2. Click in the view pane where you want the flow definition symbol to be created.
- To create a flow usage:
  1. In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the **flow** button to create a flow usage.
  2. In a view, click on the source element. A dropdown menu appears. Select the output element in the menu.
  3. Then click on the target element. A dropdown menu appears. Select the compatible input element in the menu. A flow usage is created between the elements. If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the **flow**or the **flow****def**button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.

###### Creating flow definition elements via the Textual Editor

To create flow definition elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the *flow* def keyword.
2. Name the element, then specify the flow definition features in the element body:
  1. Specify the flow definition ends by declaring the end keyword, followed by the end name and/or specialization of the needed element.
3. Click the Synchronize button. flow def FuelFlow{ 
ref :>>payload:Fuel; 
 end portfuelIn:FuelIn; 
 end portfuelOut:FuelOut; 
}

###### Creating flow definition elements via the Create Element command

To create flow definition elements via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, click flow def for flow definition.

###### Auto-creating flow definition elements via the Extract Definition command

To auto-create a flow definition element via the Extract Definition command

- See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Extract Definition and Usage' space='']page.

##### Setting the payload for the flow

While the payload name and sign are displayed on the flow in a view by default, you can hide them using the **Show Payload** [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modifying styles of individual symbols' space=''].

###### Setting the payload for the flow via the Textual Editor

To set the payload for the flow via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor before the keyword*from*within the flow for which you want to set a payload.
2. Declare the keyword of followed by either of the following:
  1. The name of the payload specialized by a definition element.
  2. The name of the definition element you want to set as payload for the flow.
3. Click the Synchronize button. flow:FuelFlowofFuelfromfuelTank.fuelOuttoengine.fuelIn; // specialized(feature typing) flow with payload set as a definition elementflow: FuelFlowof flowingFuel :FuelfromfuelTank.fuelOutto engine.fuelIn; // specialized(feature typing) flow with a named and specialized (feature typing) payloadflow of Fuel from fuelTank.fuelOutto engine.fuelIn; // unspecialized flowwith payload set as a definition element

###### Setting the payload for the flow via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To set the payload for the flow via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Drag and Drop handler' space=''] on a flow element symbol executing the **Set Payload** **** command.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

A flow is a transfer that continues while the source and target actions execute. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=269910147 space=SYSML2P version=17 -->
## PAGE 00334: (2026x) Forward compatibility

- page_id: `269910147`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/269910147/2026x+Forward+compatibility
- version_number: 17
- version_date: `2026-06-26T11:51:17.190+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Getting started
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The cross-version compatibility checking mechanism is triggered when a project with new feature usages is loaded with an older version of the modeling tool. The mechanism tracks feature usages of the 2026x Refresh1 and later Refresh versions, which results in the appearance of a **Missing features detected** notification in earlier modeling tool versions. The following table shows tracked features and the version they appear in. Projects can be committed/saved without any consequences.

| Feature name | Version |
| --- | --- |
| Hierarchical table display mode and its persistence in the model. | 2026x Refresh1 |
| Table column controls, including column order, sorting, width, and pinning persistence. | 2026x Refresh1 |
| Tabular view columns with user-defined features. | 2026x Refresh1 |
| Tabular views defined by an abstract view definition. | 2026x Refresh1 |
| Referencing elements' (e.g., perform, include, satisfy, verify, etc.) representation as paths in views. | 2026x Refresh1 |

**Compatibility of profiles**

While the tool is backward compatible, we highly recommend that every user working on a particular project use the same version of the tool. For example, if you use an older version of the tool and open a project created with a later version, a balloon notification pops up:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To avoid this:

- Make sure that every user working on the same project uses the same version of the tool.
- Do not migrate profiles unless every user working on the same project is informed and ready for migration.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The cross-version compatibility checking mechanism is triggered when a project with new feature usages is loaded with an older version of the modeling tool. The mechanism tracks feature usages of the 2026x Refresh1 and later Refresh versions, which results in the appearance of a <strong>Missing features detected</strong> notification in earlier modeling tool versions. The following table shows tracked features and the version they appear in. Projects can be committed/saved without any consequences. </p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 55.225%;"><colgroup class=""><col class="" style="width: 79.0176%;" /><col class="" style="width: 20.9401%;" /></colgroup><tbody class=""><tr class=""><td class="highlight-#f4f5f7" data-highlight-colour="#f4f5f7"><strong title="">Feature name</strong></td><td class="highlight-#f4f5f7" data-highlight-colour="#f4f5f7"><strong title="">Version</strong></td></tr><tr class=""><td><p>Hierarchical table display mode and its persistence in the model. </p></td><td>2026x Refresh1</td></tr><tr><td><p>Table column controls, including column order, sorting, width, and pinning persistence. </p></td><td>2026x Refresh1</td></tr><tr><td><p>Tabular view columns with user-defined features.</p></td><td>2026x Refresh1</td></tr><tr><td><p>Tabular views defined by an abstract view definition.</p></td><td>2026x Refresh1</td></tr><tr><td><p>Referencing elements' (e.g., perform, include, satisfy, verify, etc.) representation as paths in views. </p></td><td>2026x Refresh1</td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>Compatibility of profiles</strong></p><p>While the tool is backward compatible, we highly recommend that every user working on a particular project use the same version of the tool. For example, if you use an older version of the tool and open a project created with a later version, a balloon notification pops up:</p><p><ac:image ac:height="200"><ri:attachment ri:filename="balloon_error.png" /></ac:image></p><p>To avoid this:</p><ul><li data-uuid="924ea361-f0ca-4aca-a4c7-8b38d508048f">Make sure that every user working on the same project uses the same version of the tool.</li><li data-uuid="2a0cb650-985a-4bbd-94c4-ffb030e652f4">Do not migrate profiles unless every user working on the same project is informed and ready for migration.</li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=263094376 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00335: (2026x) Frame concern

- page_id: `263094376`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/263094376/2026x+Frame+concern
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-12-11T19:20:54.061+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Requirements > (2026x) Concerns
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A framed concern usage is a subrequirement usage, which will automatically be considered a required constraint of its containing requirement definition or usage.

There are two ways concerns can be framed:

50%

- by referencing a concern defined elsewhere in the model.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the frame keyword (unnamed element) or the frame concern keyword (named element). 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - See the procedures:
    - :
      - 
      - 
      - 

50%

- by containing an owned frame concern usage, i.e., the framed concern is the frame concern usage itself.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the frame concern keyword. [ATTACHMENT filename='frame concern 1.png']
  - See the procedures:
    - :
      - 
      - 
      - 

##### Framing a concern by referencing a concern defined elsewhere in the model

###### Framing concerns via the Textual Editor

To frame concerns via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of the element that you want to frame a concern, do one of the following:
  1. For an unnamed frame concern, declare the frame keyword followed by the name of the framed concern.
  2. For a named frame concern, declare the frame concern keyword, specify the element name, then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Reference subsetting' space=''] the framed concern.
2. Specify the concern body.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

concernbrakingConcern{subjectvehicle:Vehicle;stakeholderdriver:Person;attributemaxBrakingDistance:DistanceValue;}

requirementbrakingRequirement{ 
subjectvehicle:Vehicle; 
actorenvironment:'Driving Environment'; 
attributespeedLimit:SpeedValue; 
attributemaxBrakingDistance:DistanceValue;

framebrakingConcern{ // step 1.a - unnamed usage frames another concern usage via reference subsetting 
:>>maxBrakingDistance=brakingRequirement::maxBrakingDistance; 
}

frame concernbrakingRequirementConcern ::> brakingConcern{ // step 1.b - named usage frames another concern usage via reference subsetting 
 :>> maxBrakingDistance = brakingRequirement::maxBrakingDistance; 
 } 
}

###### Framing concerns via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To frame concerns via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop a concern element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Drag and Drop handler' space=''] on the needed element symbol, executing the Create Frame Concern command.

###### Framing concerns via the Specification panel

To frame concerns via the Specification panel

- Drag and drop a concern element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's Drag and Drop handler and drop it on the Required Constraint field in the Properties section of the Specification panel for the frame concern usage. The appropriate concern is specified for the element.

##### Framing a concern via the owned frame concern usage itself as the framed concern

###### Framing concerns via the Textual Editor1Framing concerns via the Textual Editor

To frame concerns via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of the element that you want to frame a concern, declare the frame concern keyword followed by its name.
2. Specify the concern body.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

requirementbrakingRequirement{ 
subjectvehicle:Vehicle; 
actorenvironment:'Driving Environment'; 
attributespeedLimit:SpeedValue; 
attributemaxBrakingDistance:DistanceValue;

frameconcernbrakingRequirementConcern{ // named framed concern 
attributemaxBrakingDistance:DistanceValue; 
:>>maxBrakingDistance=brakingRequirement::maxBrakingDistance; 
} 
}

###### Framing concerns via the symbol compartment in a view

To frame concerns via the symbol compartment in a view

1. Do one of the following:
  1. Select a requirement symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, click the frame concern element.
  2. Select a requirement symbol and on the right side of the frames compartment, click the Create frame concern button. The new feature is created and displayed in its appropriate compartment.
2. Specify the concern. The new element is created and displayed in the frames compartment.

###### Framing concerns via the Specification panel2Framing concerns via the Specification panel

To frame concerns via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space=''].
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the **frame concern**element in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the **frame concern**compartment section's bar, click the **Create frame concern**[IMAGE alt='' src=''] button.
3. Specify the concern. The element is created in the frames compartment.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
